title: The Three-Step Workflow
text: Every GDS analysis follows a straightforward pattern: Project, Run, and Write. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s017-000-existing] Let's take a look at this diagram. This workflow is designed to keep your source data separate from your analysis. This means you can experiment quickly and iteratively on an in-memory version of your graph without altering the original. Now, you might wonder why we sometimes say 'two or three steps.' That's because you have the flexibility to run an algorithm and write its results in one go, or you can chain multiple algorithm results together before writing them back to the graph.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to apply the Project, Run, Write workflow to any GDS analysis. You'll learn how to create graph projections using Cypher projection syntax, run algorithms in various execution modes like stats, stream, mutate, and write, and manage in-memory projections by listing and dropping graphs. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s018-000-new] As you can see in this flowchart, each step of the workflow is highlighted and annotated with its purpose in the GDS analysis process.

title: Why This Workflow?
text: This workflow is all about speed, safety, and flexibility. In-memory projections are much faster than querying the database directly. They also keep your source graph safe, as it remains unchanged until you decide to write results back. Plus, you can run multiple algorithms on the same projection to compare and combine results. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s019-001-new] Notice how this illustration compares in-memory projections with direct database queries, highlighting these advantages.

title: Step 1: Project
text: The first step in the GDS workflow is projecting your graph. This involves creating an in-memory structure that contains nodes and relationships, just like your main graph. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s020-001-existing] Here, you can see a representation of a main graph with an arrow pointing to its projected variant. The main graph stays on disk, while the projected graph is stored in memory. This in-memory graph is optimized for topology and property lookup operations.

title: Projecting graphs with Cypher
text: You can create a projection of your graph using Cypher, or with a native projection. A Cypher projection follows the same pattern as any other Cypher query you're used to running. However, instead of returning results, the `gds.graph.project()` procedure creates an in-memory representation based on the `MATCH` clause. Let's break down the Cypher projection example. It has three components: First, the Cypher query, where the `MATCH` clause defines which nodes and relationships to include. Second, the projection call, where the `gds.graph.project()` procedure creates the projection with a unique name and defines the source and target nodes. Finally, the return statement, where the `RETURN` clause provides metadata about the created projection, aliased as `g`.

title: Using Native projections
text: Now, let's talk about native projections. They produce the same result as Cypher projections but are more concise, though less flexible. To create a native projection, use the `CALL` keyword to invoke the `gds.graph.project()` procedure and `YIELD` the metadata. The native projection loads the provided labels into the project, connects those nodes with the specified relationship types, and provides the same metadata, accessed using the `YIELD` clause.

title: Cypher vs Native
text: In the early stages of this workshop, we'll focus on Cypher projection. Later, as you become familiar with the Python driver, we'll switch to using Native projection.

title: Running algorithms
text: Once you've projected your graph into memory, you can run algorithms on it using the `CALL gds.<algorithm>.<mode>` command. Let's look at an example of running an algorithm. Here, the degree centrality algorithm is called in stream mode. It's run on the `actors-graph` projection, and a map of configuration options can be provided as the third argument. Each algorithm yields a unique set of results, which can be processed with Cypher.

title: Execution modes
text: There are five execution modes for algorithm commands: Stats, Stream, Mutate, Write, and Estimate. Stats mode gives you summary statistics without viewing individual results. Stream mode lets you view results directly without storing them. Mutate mode stores results in the projection. Write mode persists results to your database. And Estimate mode checks memory requirements before running. You can invoke each of these modes by appending the mode to the algorithm name. For example, to invoke the stats mode, you would use `CALL gds.degree.stats('actor-graph-native')`.

title: Estimate mode
text: The estimate mode is particularly useful for estimating the memory requirements for an algorithm. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s026-007-new] This diagram illustrates the estimate mode, showing input parameters and output metrics like nodeCount and requiredMemory. Throughout this workshop, you'll learn how and when to use each of these modes.

title: Write from algorithm
text: Ultimately, you'll end most GDS sessions by writing your results back to the graph. The `CALL gds.<algorithm>.write` command will run the algorithm directly and then write the results. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s027-008-new] Here's a flowchart showing the process of writing algorithm results back to the graph, detailing steps and expected outcomes.

title: Write from projection
text: You can also write results to your graph projection first using mutate mode. This allows you to store results in the projection before writing them back to the main graph.

title: Write from graph
text: After storing results in the projection, you can write them from the graph projection back to your main graph. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s029-010-new] This flowchart illustrates the process of writing node properties from a projection back to the main graph.

title: List graphs
text: Even when you've finished working on a projection, it will remain in memory until you either stop the server or explicitly drop it. You can see which graphs you have in memory with the `gds.graph.list()` command. This is a handy way to keep track of your in-memory graphs.

title: Dropping graphs
text: Once you're done with a projection, you can drop it from memory using the `CALL gds.graph.drop('actors-graph')` command. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s031-012-new] This illustration shows the process and implications of dropping a graph from memory, emphasizing the importance of writing data back first. Remember, the entire graph will disappear, including any data within it, so always ensure to write important information back to your main graph before dropping.

title: Drop all
text: Sometimes, you might end up with several graphs in memory. You can drop them all at once with a simple pattern. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-2-gds-workflow-s032-013-new] This flowchart depicts the process of dropping all graphs from memory, including steps and expected results.

title: Lesson Summary
text: In this lesson, you got to grips with the general GDS workflow: Project, Run, Write. In the next lesson, you'll learn how to project a graph and run your first graph projection.
